home.title: Wepisia
home.quote: “Knowledge is like a baobab: no one person can embrace it.”
home.twoByTwoDesc: Contingency analysis, odds ratio, relative risk.
home.biostatisticsDesc: From power calculations to Mantel‑Haenszel tests, access a suite of scientifically validated tools.
home.aiDescription: Automatically interpret your results using our contextual intelligence engine.
home.geospatialDescription: Visualize epidemiological events on interactive maps with dynamic heatmaps and intelligent clusters.
home.simulationDescription: Simulate complex epidemiological scenarios with our advanced simulation engine for optimal performance.

command.searchPlaceholder: Search for a module or test…

settings.title: Settings
settings.customizeApp: Customize the application according to your preferences
settings.reduceAnimationsDescription: Disables or simplifies transitions and animations
settings.highContrastDescription: Increases color contrast for better readability
settings.compactModeDescription: Reduces margins and padding to display more content
settings.enableNotificationsDescription: Display toast notifications in the application
settings.soundNotificationsDescription: Play a sound when a notification appears
settings.hapticFeedbackDescription: Vibrate the device on notifications (if supported)
settings.settingsSaved: Settings are automatically saved in your browser.
settings.resetConfirmMessage: All your custom settings will be lost. This action cannot be undone.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Epidemiology and mortality statistics.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small numbers (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid‑P).
stdMortalityCalculator.jstatMissing: jStat not detected - exact intervals and p‑values replaced with approximations.
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: SMR Calculator - Statistical tool for epidemiology
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The SMR compares observed deaths in a specific population to expected deaths if that population had the same mortality rates as the general population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: The CI indicates the reliability of the result. If it contains the value 1, the result is not statistically significant.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Based on the Poisson distribution and the χ² law. Reference for small numbers.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Continuity correction of the exact test, less conservative.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Rothman & Boice (1979) approximation, very accurate even for O > 5.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpChi2Desc: (O‑E)²/E, compares fit to the Poisson distribution.

introduction.subtitle: Complete guide to biostatistics and simulation modules.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Epidemiology and mortality statistics.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small numbers (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid‑P).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationSignifHigh: The excess is statistically significant (CI entirely > 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationSignifLow: The reduction is statistically significant (CI entirely < 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is the ratio between the number of observed deaths in the studied population and the number of deaths theoretically expected if this population had the same mortality rates as the reference population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: A CI that does not include 1.0 indicates a statistically significant difference at the chosen level.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Garwood's exact CI (1936) based on the χ² law. Two-sided exact Poisson test.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Lancaster's Mid-P test (1961). Less conservative than the exact test. CI calculated by numerical bisection.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Byar's cubic approximation (Rothman & Boice, 1979). Reliable for obs ≥ 1. p-value by CI inversion.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpVdbDesc: Vandenbroucke's square root transformation (1982). p-value: z = 2(√obs − √exp).
stdMortalityCalculator.helpRgDesc: Log-normal CI: exp(ln(SMR) ± z/√obs). Rothman & Greenland (1998). Undefined for obs = 0.
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: Automatically generated report — for epidemiological purposes only.

twoByTwoTable.title: 2×2 Tables
twoByTwoTable.description: Assessment of association between exposure and disease.
twoByTwoTable.explanation: 2×2 tables cross-tabulate an exposure (yes/no) with a disease (yes/no). The statistics produced include Fisher's exact and mid-p tests, chi‑square tests, odds ratio, relative risk, risk difference, and etiologic fractions.

proportions.title: Proportions
proportions.description: Estimation of a proportion and its confidence interval.
proportions.exactClopperPearson: • Exact (Clopper-Pearson): Conservative, based on the binomial distribution
proportions.midPExact: • Exact Mid-P: Less conservative than Clopper-Pearson
proportions.useCaseDescription: Out of 500 people tested, 75 are positive. What is the true prevalence?

proportionsSample.title: Sample size for a proportion
proportionsSample.description: Calculates the sample size needed to estimate a proportion.
proportionsSample.explanation: This module determines the number of subjects required to estimate a proportion with a given precision.
proportionsSample.exampleDescription: To estimate a prevalence of 20% with a precision of ±3% and a 95% confidence level:

oneRate.title: Single rate test
oneRate.description: Inference on an incidence or mortality rate.
oneRate.explanation: Compares an observed rate to a reference rate or calculates its confidence interval. Uses the Poisson distribution for rare events.
oneRate.exampleDescription: In a cohort of 10,000 person-years, 25 cases are observed.

compareTwoRates.title: Comparison of two rates
compareTwoRates.description: Test for equality of two incidence rates.
compareTwoRates.explanation: Compares two rates based on person-time data. Calculates the rate ratio and rate difference with their confidence intervals, as well as the Mantel-Haenszel test.

anova.title: One-way ANOVA
anova.description: Comparison of means of three or more groups.
anova.explanation: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) tests the null hypothesis that the means of several groups are equal. It partitions total variance into between-group and within-group components.
anova.anovaTable: ANOVA table: Sum of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares

tTest.title: Student's t-test
tTest.explanation: The t-test compares the means of two independent groups. Two versions are provided: Student's t-test (equal variances) and Welch's t-test (unequal variances).

meanConfidenceInterval.title: Confidence interval for a mean
meanConfidenceInterval.description: Estimation of a population mean from a sample.
meanConfidenceInterval.explanation: Calculates the confidence interval for a mean from sample descriptive statistics (n, mean, standard deviation). Uses Student's t-distribution.

meanDifferencePower.title: Power for a difference of means
meanDifferencePower.description: Calculates the power of a test comparing means.
meanDifferencePower.explanation: Power is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false. This module calculates it from sample sizes, expected difference, and variability.

meanDifferenceSample.title: Sample size for difference of means
meanDifferenceSample.description: Determines the required number of subjects.
meanDifferenceSample.explanation: Calculates the sample size required to detect a given difference between two means, with specified power and confidence level.

medianPercentileCi.title: Confidence interval for median and percentiles
medianPercentileCi.explanation: Calculates confidence intervals for the median and percentiles (25th, 75th, etc.) using the binomial or rank-based method.

unmatchedCaseControl.title: Unmatched case-control analysis
unmatchedCaseControl.description: Analysis of exposure-disease association in case-control studies.
unmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module analyzes data from an unmatched case-control study. It calculates the odds ratio (OR), its 95% confidence interval, and performs the Mantel-Haenszel chi‑square test to test association.

sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.title: Sample size (unmatched case-control)
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.description: Calculates the number of cases and controls needed.
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module calculates the sample size for an unmatched case-control study. Users enter the desired confidence level, power, proportion exposed among controls, and either an odds ratio or the proportion exposed among cases.

matchedCaseControl.title: Matched case-control analysis
matchedCaseControl.explanation: For matched case-control studies, this module calculates the Mantel-Haenszel odds ratio, confidence interval, and McNemar's test. It handles 1:1, 1:n, and M:N matching.

cohort.title: Cohort study analysis
cohort.description: Calculation of relative risk, risk difference, and attributable fractions.
cohort.explanation: In cohort studies, exposed and unexposed groups are followed over time. This module calculates the relative risk (RR), risk difference (RD), and etiological fractions among the exposed and in the population.

rct.title: Randomized controlled trial (RCT)
rct.description: Specific analysis for randomized clinical trials.
rct.explanation: Module dedicated to randomized trials. Calculates relative risk, absolute risk reduction (ARR), number needed to treat (NNT), and corresponding confidence intervals.

screening.title: Diagnostic test evaluation
screening.description: Sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, likelihood ratios.
screening.explanation: This module evaluates a diagnostic test's performance against a gold standard. It calculates sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, likelihood ratios, Youden's index, and Cohen's Kappa coefficient.

doseResponse.title: Dose-response (χ² trend)
doseResponse.description: Linear trend test for more than two exposure levels.
doseResponse.explanation: This module performs the Mantel-Haenszel linear trend chi‑square test for data with more than two exposure levels (e.g., increasing doses). It calculates the 1-degree-of-freedom p-value to test for a linear relationship between exposure and risk.
doseResponse.multipleStrata: Multiple strata can be entered to control for confounders.

rByCTable.title: R x C Table
rByCTable.description: Analysis of contingency tables of any size.
rByCTable.explanation: This module generalizes the analysis of 2×2 tables to tables of dimension R (rows) × C (columns). It calculates Pearson's χ², the likelihood ratio (G²), the contingency coefficient, and Cramer's V. It can also analyze multiple strata.
rByCTable.associationBloodGroupDisease: Association between blood group and disease (3 groups × 4 phenotypes)

randomNumbers.title: Random number generator
randomNumbers.description: Generation of random numbers for randomization and simulations.
randomNumbers.explanation: This module generates random numbers from different distributions: uniform, normal, binomial, Poisson. It can also generate sequences for clinical trial randomization (permuted blocks).

simulation.description: Simulate the spread of an infectious disease across interconnected regions, using compartmental models and interventions.
simulation.explanation1: This module allows simulating the evolution of an epidemic across a network of regions (e.g., countries, departments). Each region has its own population and compartments (S, E, I, R, D, Q depending on the chosen model). Regions are connected by links modeling population flows (mobility), influencing inter‑regional dynamics.
simulation.explanation2: Calculations use ordinary differential equations (ODE) in discrete time. The user can adjust epidemiological parameters, add interventions (lockdown, vaccination), and observe the impact in real time on an interactive map, graphs, or a dynamic network.
simulation.sir: SIR : S, I, R - Basic model without incubation period
simulation.seir: SEIR : S, E, I, R - Adds an exposed phase (E)
simulation.seird: SEIRD : S, E, I, R, D - Includes mortality (D)
simulation.seiqrd: SEIQRD : S, E, I, Q, R, D - Adds quarantine (Q)
simulation.sigmaIncubation: σ (incubation) : 0.2 - rate of transition E → I
simulation.interactiveMap: Interactive map : 2D (Leaflet) or 3D (Globe) with markers proportional to population and mobility flows.
simulation.evolutionCurves: Evolution curves : Tracking of S, E, I, R, D compartments over time. Ability to compare two simulations.
simulation.dynamicGraph: Dynamic graph : Force-directed visualization, with nodes colored according to infection rate, links weighted by mobility.
simulation.detailedData: Detailed data : Complete list of regions with compartment counts and infection rate.
simulation.massVaccination: Mass vaccination : β reduced, 40% efficacy at day 30
simulation.lateIntervention: Late intervention : lockdown at day 50, R₀=3.0
simulation.cumulativeInterventions: Multiple interventions can be cumulative (multiplicative effect).
simulation.map: Map : Type : 2D (Leaflet) / 3D (Globe) | Theme : light, dark, satellite | Marker shape : circle / icon | Line opacity (mobility)
simulation.graphs: Graphs : Line style : solid / dashed | Adjustable thickness | Fill opacity under curves | Grid show/hide | Annotations for interventions
simulation.network: Network : Node shape : circle, square, triangle | Link style : solid / dashed | Repulsion force (charge) | Clustering by region (based on cluster attribute)
simulation.step1: Select a model (SEIRD by default) and a scenario (Base).
simulation.step2: Click Start to launch the simulation. Observe the evolution on the map and graphs.
simulation.step3: Add an intervention (lockdown) via the dedicated button to see its impact on the infected curve.
simulation.step4: Switch to comparison mode to simulate an alternative scenario with different parameters and compare curves.
simulation.keyboardShortcuts: Keyboard shortcuts (3D map mode): arrows to rotate the globe, R to recenter, 2/3 to change projection.
simulation.technicalNote: Note: The simulation uses a discrete time step of 1 day. Equations are based on standard compartmental models. Inter‑region mobility is modeled by a diffusion term proportional to the prevalence difference.

geospatial.description: Based on Leaflet and react-leaflet - explore your epidemiological data on dynamic maps.
geospatial.explanation1: The geospatial module allows loading, visualizing, and analyzing georeferenced data (points, incidents, incidence rates...). It offers three complementary representation modes: individual markers, clustering for large volumes, and heatmap to visualize densities.
geospatial.explanation2: Data can be imported from CSV or Excel files, or via built-in examples (historical epidemics, continental data). An integrated AI analysis automatically provides summaries, trends, and alerts.
geospatial.dataImport: Data import : Drag and drop or select your files. Automatic detection of latitude/longitude columns and intensity value.
geospatial.multiLayers: Multi‑layers : Each dataset is an independent layer. Show/hide, change color, radius, or opacity for each series.
geospatial.integratedAIAnalysis: Integrated AI analysis : Automatically generate a summary, trends, recommendations, and risk alerts from visible data.
geospatial.individualPoints: Individual points : Each data point is represented by a circle whose size and color vary according to intensity (e.g., number of cases). Ideal for small datasets.
geospatial.pointsParameters: Parameters : global radius, opacity, color per dataset
geospatial.clustering: Clustering : Automatically groups nearby points into clusters whose size indicates the number of elements. Recommended for more than 500 points.
geospatial.clusteringOptions: Options : cluster color, text color, max cluster radius, disable threshold on zoom
geospatial.heatmap: Heatmap : Visualizes point density weighted by intensity. Ideal for identifying clusters or risk areas.
geospatial.heatmapSettings: Settings : radius, blur, color gradient, minimum opacity
geospatial.heatmapConfig: Heatmap : Radius - heat point size (default 25) | Blur - gradient smoothing (default 15) | Minimum opacity - transparency of cold zones | Customizable gradient - e.g., 0.4 → blue, 1.0 → red
geospatial.clusteringConfig: Clustering : Cluster color and text color | Max cluster radius (grouping distance) | Disable threshold - zoom level at which clusters display as individual points | Spiderfy - spider arrangement at maximum zoom
geospatial.note: Note: Each dataset retains its own visibility, clustering, heatmap, color, radius, and opacity settings. Global settings serve as defaults.
geospatial.summary: Summary : Description of clusters, correlations, global trends
geospatial.insights: Insights : Notable facts: risk area, increase, under‑reporting
geospatial.alerts: Alerts : Risk level (low / moderate / high) and points of attention
geospatial.step1: Load a CSV file containing latitude, longitude, and case columns.
geospatial.step2: Enable clustering to visualize overall distribution, then switch to heatmap to identify hotspots.
geospatial.step3: In the configuration panel, adjust dataset color, point radius, and opacity.
geospatial.step4: Click Analyze to get an AI report with risk level and recommendations.

workspace.description: Execute R code directly in your browser.
workspace.explanation1: The workspace includes a full R engine based on WebR (R port to WebAssembly). You can create, open, and save multiple .R scripts, execute all code or just one line, and immediately view results in the console or as graphics.
workspace.explanation2: The following features are available: script execution, file management, interactive console, graphics capture, image download, installation of common packages (ggplot2, dplyr, …). Some parts (advanced animation, full support for all packages) are still being improved and are marked as beta.
workspace.multiFileEditor: Multi‑file editor : Tabs to manage multiple scripts simultaneously. Rename, duplicate, delete via context menu. Auto-save in browser (localStorage).
workspace.interactiveConsoleFeature: Interactive console : Type R commands directly, navigate history with arrows, view text outputs and graphics in the same area.
workspace.graphicalVisualizations: Graphical visualizations : All graphics generated by R are automatically captured and displayed. Grid mode, animation mode, individual download, and full-screen enlargement.
workspace.packageManagement: Package management : Install packages from the webR repository (e.g., ggplot2, dplyr, KernSmooth) via the dedicated field in the header or directly with webr::install().
workspace.animationBetaDetail: Animation (beta) : If your script produces multiple graphics (e.g., in a loop), you can view them in grid mode or start an animation. The button allows play/pause animation. Manual navigation is also possible.
workspace.animationNote: animation works for pre‑generated sequences, but continuous playback of dynamic graphics is still being optimized.
workspace.packageInstallationDetail: Package installation : WebR allows installing pre‑compiled packages for WebAssembly. In the header, a dedicated field lets you quickly install a package (e.g., ggplot2). Installation logs are displayed in the console.
workspace.packageNote: Packages requiring system dependencies or native compilation may not be available.
workspace.codeExample:
# Moving epidemic curve
x <- seq(-5, 10, length.out = 200)
for (i in 1:40) {
  mu <- i / 8
  y <- dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = 1.5)
  plot(x, y, type="n", ylim=c(0, 0.3), axes=FALSE)
  polygon(c(x, rev(x)), c(y, rep(0, 200)), col="#3b82f622", border=NA)
  lines(x, y, col="#3b82f6", lwd=3)
  Sys.sleep(0.04)
}
workspace.step3: Click the Run button to execute the entire script. An animation will appear in the console.
workspace.step4: Use the navigation arrows or button to control the animation. Export an image by clicking the icon.

explorer.description: Access over 35 million biomedical references directly from your workspace.
explorer.explanation1: The explorer integrates a complete interface to the PubMed (NCBI) database. You can search by keywords, filter by year and publication type, sort by relevance or date, and view detailed abstracts.
explorer.explanation2: A unique MeSH query generation feature uses PubMed's automatic translation to enrich your search terms with appropriate MeSH descriptors. Articles can be saved locally (favorites) and exported in RIS format for your bibliography management software.
explorer.advancedSearch: Advanced search : Keywords, filters by period (e.g., 2010‑2025), article types (review, clinical trial, meta‑analysis…), sort by relevance or publication date.
explorer.meshGenerator: MeSH generator : From a free expression, retrieve the MeSH translation suggested by PubMed, including controlled terms and field tags (e.g., "[PTYP]").
explorer.favoritesExport: Favorites & export : Save up to 200 articles locally. Export a results page or all favorites in RIS format (compatible with EndNote, Zotero, Mendeley).
explorer.home: Home : Search bar with filter | Direct access to favorites via link at bottom | Display of MeSH suggestions in a separate panel
explorer.results: Results : Quick toggle between search and favorites | Side panel with article details (abstract, authors, DOI, PMID) | Temporal distribution graph of results
explorer.step1: In centered mode, type covid-19 vaccine.
explorer.step2: Click Generate MeSH to get an optimized query (e.g., "COVID-19 Vaccines"[Mesh]). Apply it.
explorer.step4: Browse results, click an article to see details. Add to favorites (bookmark icon).
explorer.step5: Switch to Favorites tab, then Export All to generate a RIS file.

analysis.title: AI Epidemiological Analysis
analysis.description: Intelligent analysis will be available soon — automatic insights, cluster detection, and epidemiological recommendations.

settings.timelineDescription: Temporal progression slider — real data to be connected.
settings.comparisonHint: Load several examples then compare their indicators.
settings.realtimeDescription: Will simulate receiving live data from a surveillance API.

map.emptyDescription: Import a CSV/Excel or load an epidemiological example via the "Examples" button.

modal.title: Epidemiological examples
modal.description: Documented real-world data — WHO, Johns Hopkins, CEREMUJER, and other organizations.

toasts.formatError: Unsupported format — CSV or Excel only

empty_state.title: Start a simulation
empty_state.description: Choose a real disease example or a scenario to launch the simulation and visualize epidemic spread.

charts.empty: Run a simulation to see graphs.

data_modal.title: Import data
data_modal.csv_label: CSV — regions (columns: id, name, population, S, E, I, R, D, latitude, longitude, color, connections, cluster)

explanation_modal.title: Guide
explanation_modal.slider_body: Drag the slider from day 0 to day 365 to visualize the epidemic state at any day. The map and dashboard update instantly. Use Play to animate progression.
explanation_modal.models_body: SIR, SEIR, SEIRD, SEIQRD. The full trajectory is precomputed when parameters change — no flickering during playback.
explanation_modal.rt_body: Rt (effective reproduction number) is calculated as R0 × S/N × (1 − reductions). Rt < 1 means the epidemic is under control.
explanation_modal.phase_body: The S-I graph plots the trajectory in S × I space. The characteristic arc shape is the signature of a well-parameterized epidemic model.
explanation_modal.interventions_body: Each intervention reduces β by a percentage, applied from the start day. Effects are cumulative (capped at 95%).
explanation_modal.comparison_body: The "Compare" button launches a parallel simulation with different parameters. Curves are overlaid.

examples_modal.title: Disease examples
examples_modal.subtitle: Parameters and initial conditions inspired by real data

covid19.name: COVID-19
covid19.description: Global pandemic. Airborne transmission, incubation period 5-6 days, CFR ~1%.

ebola2014.name: Ebola Epidemic
ebola2014.description: Major health crisis in West Africa. CFR ~50%. Transmission through direct contact.

flu_seasonal.name: Seasonal Flu
flu_seasonal.description: Classic winter epidemic. Moderate R0, fast recovery, low overall mortality.

measles.name: Measles
measles.description: Highly contagious disease (R0 12-18). Effective vaccination but insufficient coverage in some areas.

meningitis.name: Meningococcal Meningitis
meningitis.description: Seasonal epidemics in the sub-Saharan "meningitis belt". CFR ~10%.

cholera.name: Cholera
cholera.description: Epidemics linked to contaminated water. Rapid mortality without treatment, low with rehydration.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.subtitle: Wepisia
privacy.summary: Wepisia is an open‑source epidemiology and biostatistics tool. This policy explains how we handle your data.
privacy.noDataDesc: Wepisia does not collect, store, or transmit any personal data. All calculations are performed locally in your browser.
privacy.localDesc: Data entered (table values, simulation parameters) remain on your device and are erased when the tab is closed.
privacy.noTrackingDesc: No third‑party cookies, no analytics scripts, no advertising. Your privacy is fully respected.
privacy.clearLocalStorage: You can delete this data at any time via your browser settings.
privacy.dataUsageDesc: The only data you enter is used for calculations.
privacy.calculationsDesc: Values you enter (e.g., counts, parameters) are only used to generate displayed results. They are never transmitted to our servers.
privacy.persistenceDesc: No data is retained after closing the tab. You can also manually delete stored preferences.
privacy.cookiesDesc: The application does not use cookies.
privacy.cookiesDetail: Only local storage (localStorage) may be used to remember your language preferences and interface state. This data is not cookies and is never shared.
privacy.thirdPartiesDesc: No information is shared with third parties.
privacy.thirdPartiesDetail: The application contains no external scripts (analytics, advertising) and transmits no data. The only network requests are for loading necessary resources (JavaScript libraries, stylesheets).
privacy.securityDesc: Your data remains under your control.
privacy.localComputationDesc: All calculations are performed on your machine. No data leaves your browser.
privacy.httpsDesc: The site is served over HTTPS, ensuring integrity of downloaded files.
privacy.changesDesc: Any changes will be posted on this page.
privacy.changesDetail: This policy may be updated occasionally. The last update date is at the top of the page. We invite you to review it periodically.
privacy.contactDesc: For any questions regarding this policy.

about.title: About
about.subtitle: Wepisia
about.intro: A free tool for epidemiology, designed for accessibility in low-resource settings
about.visionDesc: In developing countries, resources are often limited. Wepisia was designed to meet these realities: local operation, no installation, multilingualism, and open code (MIT).
about.openEpiDesc: OpenEpi revolutionized access to epidemiological calculations by making them free, online, and accessible to all. Released under the MIT license, it remains a global reference.
about.commitmentDesc: Wepisia is and will remain free, open source (MIT), and multilingual. Developed by Xcept-Health, it embodies our conviction: public health tools must be accessible without barriers.
about.openEpiHeritageDesc: Context, objectives, and features of the original project.
about.openEpiContext: OpenEpi was born from the observation that commercial software did not always meet the needs of epidemiologists. The team aimed to provide modern, documented, and tested programs, using a common interface, working via the web or locally, platform-independent, and open source.
about.openEpiGoals: Objectives: provide modern, platform-independent, open-source programs, facilitate interaction between epidemiologists and developers.
about.feature1: Stratified tables, screening tests, diagnostic method evaluation
about.feature2: Power calculations for cohort studies, case-control studies, clinical trials
about.feature3: Random number generator, χ² trend test (dose‑response)
about.feature4: Saving result files and loading example data
about.genesisText: Initiated by F. Ariel Shadrac OUEDRAOGO, a medical student and self-taught developer from Burkina Faso, Wepisia is an African evolution of OpenEpi. Entirely redesigned with modern technologies (React, Vite, Tailwind), it brings:
about.featureSimulation: Advanced multi‑region simulation (SIR, SEIR, SEIRD, SEIQRD)
about.featureMultilingual: Full multilingual support (Mooré, Wolof, Hausa, Swahili, French, English)
about.recognitionDesc: We express our deep gratitude to the creators of OpenEpi
about.recognitionConclusion: Without their pioneering work, Wepisia would never have come to be. Their source code, available under the MIT license, remains the scientific foundation on which our project rests.
about.contributeDesc: Developers, epidemiologists, translators, designers: all contributions are welcome!

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.subtitle: Wepisia
terms.summary: Please read these terms carefully before using Wepisia. By accessing or using the application, you agree to be bound by these terms.
terms.freeAndOpenDesc: Wepisia is provided free of charge under the MIT license. You are free to use, modify, and distribute the code.
terms.openSourceDesc: The complete source code is available on GitHub, ensuring full transparency and the possibility of community contributions.
terms.noWarrantyDesc: The software is provided 'as is', without any warranty. Use at your own risk.
terms.acceptanceDesc: By using Wepisia, you accept these terms.
terms.acceptanceDetail: These Terms of Use govern your use of the Wepisia application, including all content, features, and services. If you do not agree to any of these terms, you must not use the application.
terms.useDesc: Wepisia is intended for educational, research, and professional purposes in epidemiology and biostatistics.
terms.permittedTeaching: Public health training and capacity building
terms.ipDetail: The source code is under the MIT license. You are free to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell copies of the software, subject to the terms of the MIT license. The names 'Wepisia', 'Xcept-Health' and associated logos are registered trademarks of Xcept-Health.

terms.disclaimerDetail: Wepisia is provided without any warranty, express or implied, including, but not limited to, warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, or non-infringement. The entire risk as to the quality and performance of the software is with you.

notFound.title: Lost in space?
notFound.message: This page has drifted off our radar. Use your instruments to return to safe harbor.
notFound.modalText: These coordinates lead to the Martyrs' Memorial in Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso - a memorial to those who sacrificed their lives for freedom. A place of memory and hope.

twoByTwo.title: 2×2 Table
twoByTwo.description: Calculate odds ratio, relative risk, and chi‑square tests.
twoByTwo.increasedRisk: Relative risk indicates increased risk among exposed.
twoByTwo.protectiveEffect: Relative risk indicates a protective effect of exposure.
twoByTwo.noAssociation: Relative risk is 1, suggesting no association.
twoByTwo.ciNote: CIs for OR and RR are calculated by Taylor series approximation (Woolf method).
twoByTwo.orNote: CIs calculated by Taylor series (Woolf method). AFp|OR = (a/c₁)·(OR−1)/OR.
twoByTwo.principleText: Analysis of a 2×2 table allows studying the association between an exposure and a disease. The odds ratio (case‑control studies) and relative risk (cohort studies) are calculated, along with various independence tests.
twoByTwo.fisherDesc: Based on the hypergeometric distribution, recommended for small numbers.
twoByTwo.interpretationText: If the confidence interval of OR or RR excludes 1, the association is significant. The p‑value of the χ² test indicates the significance of the association (p < 0.05).

doseResponse.title: Dose-Response Analysis
doseResponse.description: Linear trend test for different exposure levels.
doseResponse.reportFooter: Dose-Response Calculator - Statistical tool for epidemiology
doseResponse.principleText: This test evaluates whether there is a linear trend between exposure level (dose) and disease risk. It is used in case-control or cohort studies with several ordered exposure levels.
doseResponse.methodsText: For each level, odds ratio and relative risk are calculated relative to the reference level (lowest). The trend test is an extension of the χ² test (Mantel), with a statistic distributed as χ² with 1 degree of freedom.
doseResponse.interpretationText: A p-value < 0.05 indicates a significant trend. The direction (positive/negative) indicates whether risk increases or decreases with dose.

rByC.title: R×C Table
rByC.description: Analysis of contingency tables of arbitrary size.
rByC.significantInterpretation: The p-value of {{p}} indicates a significant association between variables (p < 0.05). Cramer's V of {{v}} measures the strength of this association.
rByC.nonSignificantInterpretation: The p-value of {{p}} does not indicate a significant association between variables (p ≥ 0.05). Cramer's V of {{v}} suggests a weak or non-existent association.
rByC.principleText: Pearson's chi-square (χ²) test is used to determine whether there is a statistically significant association between two categorical variables. It compares observed frequencies in an R×C contingency table to expected frequencies under the hypothesis of independence.
rByC.independenceDesc: The two variables are not related.
rByC.dependenceDesc: There is an association between them.
rByC.expectedCountsDesc: Less than 20% of cells should have an expected count less than 5, and no expected count should be less than 1. If this condition is not met, consider an exact test (e.g., Fisher's test for 2×2 tables).
rByC.unpairedDataDesc: Each subject contributes to only one cell.
rByC.chiSquareDesc: Sum of (observed - expected)² / expected. The higher the value, the greater the deviation from independence.
rByC.dfDesc: (number of rows - 1) × (number of columns - 1). They determine the shape of the χ² distribution under H₀.
rByC.pValueDesc: Probability of observing a χ² value as extreme (or more) under H₀. If p < 0.05 (usual threshold), H₀ is rejected at the 5% risk level.
rByC.cramersVDesc: Measure of association strength, ranging from 0 (independence) to 1 (perfect dependence).
rByC.complementaryMethodsDesc: For 2×2 tables, other tests may be more appropriate:
rByC.fisherExactDesc: Recommended when expected counts are small (<5).
rByC.yatesCorrectionDesc: Sometimes applied to χ² for 2×2 tables, making the test more conservative.
rByC.jStatNote: This calculator uses the jStat library for exact p‑values from the χ² distribution.
rByC.exampleText: Suppose we study the relationship between smoking (smoker / non‑smoker) and lung cancer occurrence (yes / no). A 2×2 table could be:
rByC.exampleInterpretation: The χ² test would give a p-value < 0.001, indicating a significant association. Cramer's V would be about 0.45, a moderate strength.

screening.title: Screening
screening.description: Performance analysis of a multi-level screening test.
screening.rocLabel: TPR = true positive rate, FPR = false positive rate
screening.helpPrincipleText: This module evaluates the performance of a diagnostic or screening test with multiple levels (ordinal or continuous). For each cutoff threshold, it calculates:
screening.helpEntropy: It also provides level-specific likelihood ratios and the area under the ROC curve (AUC) with its confidence interval.
screening.helpAucHighDesc: Good test - acceptable to excellent discrimination
screening.helpAucLowDesc: Poorly informative test - close to chance
screening.helpConfidenceText: All intervals are calculated using recognized methods.
screening.helpRocDesc: Points are generated by cumulating counts from the most pathological to the least pathological level. AUC is calculated by the trapezoidal method.
screening.helpLevelLRDesc: LR = (casesᵢ / non‑casesᵢ) / (total cases / total non‑cases). CI based on standard error of log(LR) using Katz formula.
screening.helpKappaMethodDesc: Measure of agreement beyond chance. CI = κ ± 1,96·SE(κ).
screening.helpEntropyMethodDesc: Percentage reduction in uncertainty (Shannon entropy) after a positive or negative test.
screening.helpBiasMethodDesc: (TP + FP - FN - TN) / N. Reflects overall classification imbalance.
screening.helpExampleText: Suppose a blood glucose test for diagnosing diabetes. Patients are classified into five glucose levels (1 = normal, 5 = very high). Data entered might be:
screening.helpExampleInterpretation: The analysis produces an ROC curve, an AUC of 0.5326 (95% CI: 0.3728 - 0.6925), and for each threshold, sensitivities, specificities, etc. One can thus choose the best trade-off.
screening.helpOrderNoteText: Levels must be entered from least pathological (first row) to most pathological (last row). This is essential for a correct ROC curve.

oneRate.title: Single Rate
oneRate.description: Analysis of a rate and its confidence interval - PersonTime1
oneRate.interpretationText: The observed rate is {{rate}} event(s) per unit person‑time. The {{level}}% confidence interval (Fisher method) ranges from {{lower}} to {{upper}}.{{precision}}
oneRate.principleText: The incidence rate (or incidence density) measures the speed at which events occur in a population followed over a period of time. It is calculated as the number of new cases divided by the sum of participation time (person‑time). Its confidence interval reflects the precision of this estimate.
oneRate.ciText: The 95% confidence interval (default) indicates the range of values within which the true rate lies with 95% probability. The narrower the interval, the more precise the estimate. If the interval contains the value 1, one cannot conclude excess or deficit risk compared to a reference population (in the case of external comparison).
oneRate.midpDesc: Continuity correction of the exact Poisson test, less conservative than Fisher's exact test. Recommended for small numbers.
oneRate.fisherDesc: Based on the χ² distribution, it is the reference method for small numbers. It guarantees a type I error risk at most equal to the nominal level.
oneRate.normalDesc: Valid for large numbers (usually a > 20). Uses standard error √a / N.
oneRate.byarDesc: Very accurate even for modest numbers (a > 5). Based on a transformation of the Poisson parameter.
oneRate.rothmanDesc: Uses the logarithm of the rate and its standard error (1/√a). The interval is then exponentiated. It ensures the lower bound remains positive.
oneRate.exampleText: In a cohort study, 33 cancer cases are observed among workers followed for 22 person‑years. The incidence rate is:
oneRate.exampleInterpretation: The 95% confidence interval by Fisher's exact method is [1.03 - 2.10] (rounded). This means the true rate has a 95% chance of being between 1.03 and 2.10 events per person‑year.

twoRates.title: Two Rates
twoRates.errorNoCases: At least one group must have cases.
twoRates.zScoreTitle: z-Score and Exact Measures of Association
twoRates.rateBasedTitle: Rate-based estimates and {{level}}% CI (per {{scale}} time unit)
twoRates.interpretationHigher: The rate in group 1 is {{percent}}% higher than in group 2.
twoRates.interpretationLower: The rate in group 1 is {{percent}}% lower than in group 2.
twoRates.helpPrincipleText: This module compares two incidence rates (cases / person‑time) by calculating the rate ratio (RR), rate difference, and their confidence intervals using several methods (exact and approximate).
twoRates.helpMidp: Miettinen (1974d): continuity correction of the exact test, recommended.
twoRates.helpFisher: Armitage (1971): based on conditional hypergeometric distribution.

meanCi.title: Confidence interval for the mean
meanCi.interpretationText: With {{level}}% confidence, the true population mean lies between {{lower}} and {{upper}} (t method recommended).
meanCi.fpcApplied: A finite population correction was applied (FPC = {{fpc}}).
meanCi.referencesNote: Methods based on OpenEpi - MeanCI. Z interval: normal approximation. t interval: Student's t distribution.
meanCi.fpcNote: Finite population correction applied if N is specified and less than 999,999,999.
meanCi.principleText: This module calculates the confidence interval for a mean from sample statistics. Two methods are provided: normal approximation (Z) and Student's t distribution, more suitable for small samples. A finite population correction can be applied if the total population size is known.
meanCi.zVsT: Z: large sample (n ≥ 30). t: small sample (n < 30) or σ unknown.
meanCi.fpcDesc: Reduces standard error when the sample covers a large part of the population.
meanCi.zDesc: CI = x̄ ± z·σ/√n, where z is the normal quantile. Usable for n ≥ 30 or σ known.
meanCi.tDesc: CI = x̄ ± t·s/√n, with t quantile of Student's t with (n‑1) df. Recommended in practice.

medianCi.title: Confidence interval for median / percentile
medianCi.description: Estimation of the population percentile rank
medianCi.interpretationText: With {{level}}% confidence, the {{percentile}}th percentile corresponds to ranks {{lowerRank}} to {{upperRank}} in a sorted sample of size {{n}}.
medianCi.howToUseSteps:
1. Sort your {{n}} observations in ascending order.
2. The {{lowerRank}}th value is the lower bound of the CI.
3. The {{upperRank}}th value is the upper bound.
With {{level}}% confidence, the true {{percentile}}th percentile lies between these two values.
medianCi.referenceNote: Method based on normal approximation of ranks (Conover, 1999).
medianCi.helpPrincipleText: This module estimates the confidence interval for the rank of a population percentile, based on sample size. The normal approximation of the binomial distribution is used.
medianCi.rankIntervalDesc: The interval is given as a position in the sorted sample, not directly as a value.
medianCi.conditionDesc: The approximation is valid if n·p ≥ 5 and n·(1‑p) ≥ 5.
medianCi.intervalFormula: Interval - [max(1, floor(rank - z·SE)) , min(n, ceil(rank + z·SE))]

twoSampleTTest.title: Two-sample independent t-test
twoSampleTTest.description: Comparison of two means - Student's t-test, Welch's test, equality of variances test
twoSampleTTest.variancesDifferentAdvice: → Variances are significantly different (p < 0.05). Prefer Welch's test.
twoSampleTTest.variancesHomogeneousAdvice: → Variances are not significantly different (p >= 0.05). Student's t-test is appropriate.
twoSampleTTest.interpretationText: With {{level}}% confidence, the difference of means is {{diff}}.
twoSampleTTest.references: Student's t-test (equal variances) and Welch's t-test (Satterthwaite).
twoSampleTTest.fTestRef: Equality of variances test: Hartley's F (ratio of variances).
twoSampleTTest.helpPrincipleText: This compares the means of two independent groups based on their sizes, means, and standard deviations. Two versions of the t-test are provided: with or without the assumption of equal variances. The equality of variances test (Hartley's F) helps choose the appropriate method.
twoSampleTTest.studentMethodDesc: pooled common variance, confidence interval based on t-distribution with n₁+n₂‑2 df.
twoSampleTTest.welchMethodDesc: Welch's standard error, Satterthwaite df, corresponding confidence interval.
twoSampleTTest.fMethodDesc: F = s₁²/s₂² (largest in numerator). Two-tailed p-value.

oneWayAnova.title: One-way ANOVA
oneWayAnova.description: Analysis of variance, Fisher's F-test, Bartlett's test
oneWayAnova.minGroupsError: At least two groups are required
oneWayAnova.enterData: Enter at least two valid groups
oneWayAnova.fInterpret: Between-group variance is greater than within-group variance.
oneWayAnova.pInterpret: At least one mean differs significantly.
oneWayAnova.ssDescText: SSB = Σ nᵢ (x̄ᵢ - x̄)², SSW = Σ (nᵢ‑1)·sᵢ², SST = SSB + SSW.
oneWayAnova.bartlettDesc: Based on the logarithm of variances, follows a χ² distribution.
oneWayAnova.ciDesc: CI with own variance (t, nᵢ‑1 df) and CI with pooled variance (t, N‑k df).
oneWayAnova.helpPrincipleText: This module compares the means of several independent groups based on their sizes, means, and standard deviations. The ANOVA table partitions total variability into between-group and within-group components, and provides Fisher's F-test. Bartlett's test checks equality of variances.

sampleSizeProportion.title: Sample size for a proportion
sampleSizeProportion.subtitle: Calculates the sample size needed to estimate a proportion
sampleSizeProportion.enterData: Enter valid data to see results
sampleSizeProportion.footnote: Calculation with finite population correction. p and d are expressed as percentages.
sampleSizeProportion.helpPrinciple: This calculator determines the sample size needed to estimate a proportion with a given absolute precision, using finite population correction.
sampleSizeProportion.helpFormulaZ: Z = two-tailed normal quantile for the chosen confidence level
sampleSizeProportion.helpUsage: Enter population size (use 1,000,000 for 'infinite'), expected frequency (p), desired absolute precision (± d), and design effect (1 for simple random sampling). The table displays required sample sizes for confidence levels from 80% to 99.99%.

unmatchedCaseControl.title: Sample size - Unmatched case-control
unmatchedCaseControl.subtitle: Calculates sample size for an unmatched case-control study (Kelsey, Fleiss, Fleiss with CC)
unmatchedCaseControl.orNote: Provide either the odds ratio or the % of cases exposed; the other will be calculated automatically.
unmatchedCaseControl.enterDataHint: Check that % of controls exposed is between 0 and 100, ratio > 0, and odds ratio > 0 or % of cases exposed between 0 and 100.
unmatchedCaseControl.interpretationText: The sizes above are calculated to detect the specified odds ratio with the chosen power and confidence level. Kelsey's method uses a normal approximation with pooled variance; Fleiss uses a more precise variance formula; Fleiss with continuity correction adjusts for small sample bias. The largest size among methods is often the safest choice.
unmatchedCaseControl.references: Kelsey et al., Methods in Observational Epidemiology 2nd Ed., Table 12‑15 ; Fleiss, Statistical Methods for Rates and Proportions, formulas 3.18 & 3.19.
unmatchedCaseControl.footnote: CC = continuity correction. Results are rounded up to the next integer. Fleiss with correction uses the unrounded Fleiss sample size as a base.
unmatchedCaseControl.helpPrinciple: This calculator estimates the sample size needed for an unmatched case-control study. You specify the expected exposure proportion among controls (p0), the desired odds ratio (or the proportion among cases), the control/case ratio (r), power, and confidence level. Sizes are calculated using three common methods: Kelsey (normal approximation), Fleiss (exact variance), and Fleiss with continuity correction.
unmatchedCaseControl.helpUsage: Enter the confidence level (default 95%), power (typically 80%), control/case ratio (1 for equal groups), percentage of controls exposed (from literature), and either the odds ratio to detect or the percentage of cases exposed. Results update automatically. Use the 'Example' button to load typical values. You can copy results or export them as PDF.

cohortSampleSize.title: Sample size - Cohort / Randomized trial
cohortSampleSize.subtitle: Calculates sample size for a cohort study or randomized trial (Kelsey, Fleiss, Fleiss with CC)
cohortSampleSize.orNote: Enter one of the three (OR, RR, or RD); the others will be calculated automatically.
cohortSampleSize.enterDataHint: Check that % of unexposed is between 0 and 100, ratio > 0, and one of the parameters (OR, RR, or RD) is valid.
cohortSampleSize.interpretationText: The sizes above are calculated to detect the specified effect (OR, RR, or RD) with the chosen power and confidence level. Kelsey's method uses a normal approximation with pooled variance; Fleiss uses a more precise variance formula; Fleiss with continuity correction adjusts for small sample bias. The largest size among methods is often the safest choice.
cohortSampleSize.references: Kelsey et al., Methods in Observational Epidemiology 2nd Ed., Table 12‑15 ; Fleiss, Statistical Methods for Rates and Proportions, formulas 3.18 & 3.19.
cohortSampleSize.footnote: CC = continuity correction. Results are rounded up to the next integer. Fleiss with correction uses the unrounded Fleiss sample size as a base.
cohortSampleSize.reportSubtitle: Sample size calculator - Cohort / Randomized trial
cohortSampleSize.reportFooter: Sample size calculator - Cohort / Randomized trial
cohortSampleSize.helpPrinciple: This calculator estimates the sample size needed for a cohort study or randomized trial. You specify the proportion of events among the unexposed (p0), the desired effect (OR, RR, or RD), the ratio unexposed/exposed (r), power, and confidence level. Sizes are calculated using three common methods: Kelsey (normal approximation), Fleiss (exact variance), and Fleiss with continuity correction.
cohortSampleSize.helpUsage: Enter the confidence level (default 95%), power (typically 80%), unexposed/exposed ratio (1 for equal groups), percentage of unexposed with the outcome, and one of the three effect parameters (OR, RR, or RD). Results update automatically. Use the 'Example' button to load typical values. You can copy results or export them as PDF.

meanDiffSample.title: Sample Size - Difference of Means
meanDiffSample.subtitle: Calculates the sample size needed to detect a difference between two independent means
meanDiffSample.enterData: Enter valid parameters to see results
meanDiffSample.enterDataHint: Check that means and standard deviations are numbers, standard deviations > 0, ratio > 0, and means are different.
meanDiffSample.reportSubtitle: Sample size calculator - Difference of Means
meanDiffSample.reportFooter: Sample size calculator - Difference of Means
meanDiffSample.interpretationText: The calculated sample sizes allow the study to have {{power}}% power to detect a difference of {{delta}} between the two means, with a {{confidence}}% confidence level (two‑sided test). The total size is {{total}}. The pooled standard deviation is a weighted average of the standard deviations of the two groups.
meanDiffSample.formula:
n₁ = (z_α/₂ + z_β)² × (σ₁² + σ₂² / r) / Δ²
n₂ = r × n₁
with Δ = |μ₁ - μ₂|, r = n₂/n₁
meanDiffSample.formulaExplanation: where Δ = |μ₁ - μ₂|, r = n₂/n₁, and z are normal quantiles.
meanDiffSample.footnote: Results are rounded up to the next integer. Pooled standard deviation is calculated from estimated sample sizes.
meanDiffSample.helpPrinciple: This calculator determines the sample size needed to detect a specified difference between two independent means, based on standard deviations, confidence level, power, and allocation ratio (n₂/n₁). It uses the standard formula for two independent samples (unequal variances).
meanDiffSample.helpUsage: Enter the means and standard deviations for both groups, the confidence level (usually 95%), power (often 80%), and the sample size ratio (n₂/n₁). Use the Example button to load typical values. Results appear automatically. You can copy results or export them as PDF.

randomNumberGenerator.title: Random Number Generator
randomNumberGenerator.subtitle: Generates random numbers.
randomNumberGenerator.notesText:
Print numbers from the browser's File menu or copy and paste them into word processors, Excel, and other programs.

Numbers are generated by the JavaScript Math.random() function. Although these are pseudo-random numbers, Math.random in current browsers has been tested by many people and found to generate high-quality 'random' numbers. For more information, search the internet for 'High quality random numbers' and related topics.
randomNumberGenerator.errorMinMax: Minimum must be less than or equal to maximum
randomNumberGenerator.errorUniqueRange: Requested count exceeds available range for unique numbers
randomNumberGenerator.helpPrinciple: This generator produces random integers within a user-defined interval. The 'Unique numbers' option allows sampling without replacement, useful for samples without repetition.
randomNumberGenerator.helpUnique: each number appears at most once (requires that the requested count ≤ the size of the interval).
randomNumberGenerator.helpMethod: Generation uses JavaScript's Math.random(), which produces a pseudo-random uniform value between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). For integers, the standard formula is applied:
randomNumberGenerator.helpReference: This tool is inspired by the 'Random Numbers' module of the OpenEpi epidemiological software. For more information on random number generation in JavaScript, consult the
